meta.title: Atlantic Crossing
meta.author: Troy Wolff
meta.focusQuestion: How does Patrick Kelley's journey from Ireland to America change him?

sections.title: Leaving Dublin Bay
paragraphs: Patrick Kelley looked out over the ships floating in Dublin Bay. Below him, he saw hundreds of people crowding the docks, looking for space on one of the ships bound for America. The year was 1846, and many Irish people, most of whom were farmers, were starving. For the past two years, Ireland's potato crop had failed due to disease, resulting in a great famine.

To avoid starvation, Irish families like Patrick's bought passage on ships of all sizes sailing to the United States and Canada. The journey was dangerous, but the promise of a better life in America, where there was more land to farm and no diseases destroying the crops, drew many to the harbor. Patrick heard his father talk of jobs in big cities like New York and Boston. His father, who was a blacksmith, hoped to find a job working his craft in New York, which had thousands of horses to pull carriages and wagons through the streets.

Patrick knew his family was suffering, and he knew that America might offer a better life, yet Patrick did not want to leave Dublin. This was his home. He did not want to leave his friends, despite the food shortage, and he did not want to leave Dublin Bay, his favorite place. He often sat on the shore overlooking the bay during storms and felt the sea spray in his face, and he would listen to the stories of the suntanned sailors returning from journeys all around the world.

Patrick wanted to be a sailor in the warm South Pacific Ocean; he did not want to be a crowded passenger aboard an immigrant ship crossing the cold Atlantic. So, Patrick sat on the docks by himself, angry with his family, mostly his father, for making him leave. He shaded his eyes from the setting sun on the horizon and looked for the tall mast of the Donegal, the ship that would take his family to America the following morning. He spotted the Donegal a little farther down the docks; it was easy to pick out because it was larger than most of the other ships. He could see the name painted in gold letters across the stern of the ship as it gently bobbed in the water. Patrick glared at the massive boat, wishing it would sink to the bottom of Dublin Bay.

sections.title: Promise of a Better Life
paragraphs: By the time Patrick finally decided to go home, it had grown dark. Patrick lived on the edge of Dublin, where his house was connected to his father's large blacksmith barn and shop. After he left the edge of the water, the streets and alleyways leading home were so dark that he could see just a short way ahead.

After a few blocks, Patrick noticed footsteps behind him. The streets were usually deserted after dark, and Patrick wondered who might be out tonight. The footsteps drew closer and closer, and as he listened, Patrick detected a strange metallic clicking along with the footsteps. Frightened, Patrick kept his head down and began walking faster.

Another block later, the steps and the clicking got closer still. Frantically, Patrick tried to remember an alleyway or doorway he could duck into to get away from this stranger following him. Suddenly, Patrick felt a small jab on the top of his shoulder. His blood froze in terror. Still frightened, Patrick slowly turned around. Standing over him glared an older man with a silver beard glowing in the lamplight and a military sword in his right hand.
"Who are you?" Patrick asked, trying to sound confident. "I am First Mate Thomas O'Brien, an officer of the Donegal, at your service," he answered in a serious tone.

"But I hear the living conditions are bad on these ships," Patrick said. "Well, they often are," Thomas replied. "Some ships are worse than others. Unfortunately, conditions usually depend upon how much money a family can spend on their fare." "Your family will travel on a ship with a record for bringing its passengers safely to America, even though the journey will still be difficult and uncomfortable," O'Brien said.

Patrick thought about this for a moment. "I can't remember the last time I wasn't going to bed feeling hungry. I don't want to live like that much longer." "Exactly," Thomas responded. "In an emergency, people must make difficult choices to make life better. But listen to me, young man. I think that a better life is waiting for you and your family. You will have to work very hard, but if you do, there should be no limit to what you can accomplish."

"Of course he's right, son," Thomas answered. "Well, young man, it's getting late. You should be off to bed. After all, this may be the last peaceful night's sleep you'll have for several weeks," he said with a wink.

sections.title: Stormy Seas
paragraphs: A week later, Patrick and his family were well on their way to America aboard the Donegal. "All hands on deck! All hands on deck!" Patrick heard from above. In the darkness below decks, Patrick heard his sister groaning. She had been sick for three days, like many of the people on the ship. After several days of calm seas, the Donegal had run into a violent storm.

On the first day of the storm, Patrick watched from the deck with excitement as giant walls of water would swell and approach the ship. Patrick's stomach would rise in his throat as the Donegal climbed, hovered for one awful second at the crest of the wave, then crashed down the far side. This was exciting, but the storm was getting stronger.

"All hands on deck! We must lower these sails, boys!" Patrick heard these commands again from above. He knew that "hands" only meant the sailors, not any of the passengers, but he felt he couldn't stand staying below in the darkness one more moment. So, when no one was looking, Patrick burst up the main hatch and into the howling wind and freezing spray.

The deck of the ship was wildly confusing—sailors running around, officers barking orders, sails flapping in the wind, and white ocean spray crashing over the rails of the ship. "Excuse me, sir!" he said as he tugged on the captain's coat. The captain turned quickly and yelled, "You, boy! What are you doing above deck? Get yourself below!"

"Captain! One of the sails has wrapped itself around the upper yardarm!" a sailor yelled. "Well, send someone up there to untangle it! We need that sail down before the wind tears it!" the captain thundered. "We can't, sir," the sailor replied. "The yardarm has been damaged and can't support a sailor's weight. We would need someone much smaller."

"What? You? What makes you think you could do this?" "It's really no problem, sir. I've grown up along the coast all my life. I have to climb in the rafters of my father's blacksmith barn every day to hang up his tools." Patrick said this with as much confidence as he could fake, but the wild wind and water frightened him.
"All right, lad. Be careful, climb slowly, and do NOT look down. Just concentrate on the mast ahead of you. You can do this! Now, up you go."

Climbing the mast wasn't scary for Patrick at first, as long as he just looked straight ahead. Halfway up, however, Patrick was unable to resist temptation and looked out and below him. Through a crisscross of ropes whistling in the wind, he saw the white tops of the waves and the small heads of the sailors on deck. He felt a wave of dizziness overtake him. Fighting against this dizziness, Patrick refocused on the mast in front of him. His head quickly cleared, and he climbed the rest of the way up.

At the top, he could see the crack in the wood of the yardarm. Looking out, he could see the top of the sail flapping in the wind and the clip he needed to unhook. Carefully reaching out from the mast, Patrick stretched his hand toward the clip. Stretching a bit farther, feeling the mast sway dangerously under him, he caught hold of the clip. Tugging with all of the strength he could find, he felt the clip give way and the sail drop below him. A cheer rose from the sailors below, and the captain happily waved at him to come back down.

sections.title: Arriving in America
paragraphs: Three weeks later, Patrick stood on the deck of the Donegal with his family. Before them the buildings of New York City came into view. At last, they had made it to America! Patrick could feel the energy and bustle of this new, growing city, so different from the centuries-old calmness of Dublin.
"Why, Patrick, my boy!" he heard a familiar voice behind him. It was First Mate Thomas O'Brien. Thomas came up to Patrick and put his hand on Patrick's shoulder.

Looking at Patrick's father, Thomas said, "You know, Mr. Kelly, none of us would be here in New York right now if it weren't for your son here. What he did up on that mast in the storm was quite courageous. Quite remarkable."

"Well, we are all very proud of Patrick," Mr. Kelly said, looking fondly at his son. "And we are also very thankful to you and the rest of the crew of the Donegal. Thanks to you, my family now has a chance for a new life—a better life—here in America."

"I wish you all the luck in the world," Thomas answered. "And, Patrick, I hope to see you at the harbor whenever the Donegal is in port. You have the makings of a fine sailor, and you're welcome on the deck of this ship any time."
Patrick smiled excitedly at this invitation and imagined his future, sailing the oceans of the world, but always returning to his family and to America, his new home.

vocabulary.definition: someone who works with iron, including making horseshoes
vocabulary.sentence: <s>His father, who was a blacksmith,</s> <v>hoped</v> <o>to find a job working his craft in New York</o>.

options: someone who works with iron, including making horseshoes

vocabulary.sentence: <s>Patrick</s> <v>could feel</v> <o>the energy and bustle of this new, growing city</o>.

vocabulary.sentence: <s>The Donegal</s> <v>climbed</v>, <v>hovered</v> for one awful second at <o>the crest of the wave</o>, then <v>crashed down</v>.

options: the highest point, or top of, something

vocabulary.sentence: For the past two years, Ireland's potato crop had failed due to disease, resulting in <o>a great famine</o>.
Frantically, Patrick tried to remember an alleyway or doorway he could duck into.

options: the covering for the opening from the deck to areas below

options: a person who has come to a new country to live

options: the tools needed to build something
the qualities needed to do something
the money needed to buy something
the friends needed to help you

vocabulary.sentence: He shaded his eyes and looked for the tall mast of the Donegal.

vocabulary.sentence: <s>He</s> <v>could see</v> <o>the name painted in gold letters across the stern of the ship</o>.

problemSolution.title: The Great Irish Famine
problemSolution.context: Ireland, 1846. The potato crop has failed for two years.

problem: Ireland's potato crop failed, causing a great famine and starvation
The Donegal ship had a hole
Patrick's father lost his blacksmith tools
The sailors refused to sail to America
cause: A disease destroyed the potato plants for two years
Too many people moved to America
effect: Irish families were starving and had to leave their homeland
solution: Irish families emigrated to America where there was more land and no crop diseases
They waited for the crops to grow back
They asked other countries for food

problemSolution.title: Patrick Doesn't Want to Leave
problemSolution.context: Patrick loves Dublin Bay and doesn't want to be an immigrant passenger.

problem: Patrick was angry about leaving Dublin, his friends, and his dream of being a South Pacific sailor
cause: He loved his home and felt he had no choice but to follow his family's decision
He was afraid of the ocean
effect: Patrick sat on the docks glaring at the ship, wishing it would sink
solution: First Mate O'Brien encouraged Patrick, explaining that difficult choices lead to a better life in America
The captain gave him a job
Patrick's friends convinced him to stay

problemSolution.title: The Storm and the Tangled Sail
problemSolution.context: The Donegal hits a violent Atlantic storm. A sail wraps around a damaged yardarm.

problem: A sail wrapped around the damaged upper yardarm and was about to tear, but no sailor was small enough to climb up
cause: The violent storm damaged the yardarm so it couldn't support an adult sailor's weight
The wind blew from the wrong direction
effect: The ship was in danger because the sail couldn't be lowered in the storm
solution: Patrick volunteered to climb the mast because he was small and light from climbing barn rafters every day
The crew cut the sail loose
They waited for the storm to pass

questions.question: Which country is Patrick's homeland?
questions.evidence: Patrick Kelley looked out over the ships floating in Dublin Bay. Below him, he saw hundreds of people crowding the docks, looking for space on one of the ships bound for America. The year was 1846, and many Irish people were starving.
questions.explanation: Patrick's homeland is Ireland — he looks out over Dublin Bay and is an Irish person facing the potato famine.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose for having Patrick meet First Mate Thomas O'Brien?
questions.evidence: I think that a better life is waiting for you and your family. You will have to work very hard, but if you do, there should be no limit to what you can accomplish.
choices: To introduce a new character who encourages Patrick and changes his attitude about leaving
To let the reader know what traveling aboard a ship was like
To make Patrick want to become a sailor
questions.explanation: Thomas O'Brien serves to encourage Patrick, showing him that difficult choices can lead to a better life.

questions.question: Imagine you are Patrick and have just climbed the mast to free the sail. Write a journal entry about what it was like and how you felt afterward.
questions.explanation: This is a creative writing prompt. Think about what Patrick felt: fear, dizziness, determination, pride.
opinionPrompts: How did Patrick feel climbing the mast?
How did he feel after the sailors cheered?

questions.question: Why does Patrick's family think they would have a chance at a better life in America?
questions.evidence: The promise of a better life in America, where there was more land to farm and no diseases destroying the crops, drew many to the harbor.
questions.explanation: America offered more farmland, no potato disease, and job opportunities for Patrick's father as a blacksmith in cities like New York.
opinionPrompts: What made America seem better than Ireland?
What did Patrick's father hope to find?

ch1.title: Famine
ch1.content: A major food shortage — many people go hungry or starve. Ireland's potato crop failed for TWO years!

ch1.title: Immigrant
ch1.content: A person who comes to a new country to LIVE there permanently. Patrick's family are immigrants.

ch2.title: Frantically
ch2.content: Acting wildly with emotion — very fast and scared. Patrick walked frantically when he heard footsteps behind him!

ch2.title: Possessive Nouns
ch2.content: Ireland's potato famine — the apostrophe-s shows the famine BELONGS to Ireland. 'Farmers' crops' = apostrophe after the s for plural possessive.

ch3.title: Mast
ch3.content: The tall wooden pole on a ship that holds the sails. Patrick climbed the mast during the storm!

ch3.title: Yardarm
ch3.content: The horizontal beam that supports a sail. When it cracked, no adult sailor was light enough to climb up and fix it.

ch3.title: Possessive Nouns
ch3.content: The captain's coat = singular possessive (one captain). The sailors' commands = plural possessive (many sailors). Can you find more in Chapter 3?

ch4.title: Bustle
ch4.content: Busy and noisy activity. New York City had so much bustle — very different from quiet Dublin!

ch4.title: Makings
ch4.content: The qualities needed to do something. Thomas said Patrick has the makings of a fine sailor!

ch4.title: Problem & Solution
ch4.content: Every good story has problems and solutions. Can you identify all THREE problems Patrick faced and how each was solved?
